serving.description: 1 small serving plate (for 1–3 pax, small portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of kimchi sambal fried rice is approximately 350 g, suitable as a shared starter for 1–3 people or as a single main course portion.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, Chinese-style — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for kimchi sambal fried rice exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice (Chinese-style)' as the primary anchor (energy ~170 kcal/100g, protein ~4g, fat ~5g, carbohydrate ~26g) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy 'Rice, cooked, white, short-grain' and adjustments for kimchi (~15 kcal/100g contribution) and sambal chilli paste (~50 kcal/100g contribution). The small serving size and typical restaurant preparation inform the composite estimate. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce, kimchi brine, and sambal. All values represent a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (white rice base, Asian-style)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kimchi sambal fried rice. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Asian-style fried rice (white rice base), which reports GI values in the range 55–72 with a central estimate of approximately 64. The addition of kimchi (fermented, acidic) and sambal may modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain fried rice due to acidity and fat content; however, the white rice base dominates the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 92.75) / 100 ≈ 59.4, rounded to 60.0. Serving carbohydrate = 26.5 g/100g × 350 g = 92.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, kimchi brine, and sambal chilli paste
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 1680 mg per small serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced soy sauce and sambal is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 64) and high carbohydrate load from white rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in small portions and pair it with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the glycemic response, as the white rice base contributes a medium GI of approximately 64 and a high glycemic load per serving of approximately 60.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content including saturated fat from egg and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should be cautious with this dish due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat contribution; occasional consumption in small portions is preferable, and preparation with less oil and reduced-sodium soy sauce would improve its cardiovascular profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kimchi Sambal Fried Rice (Small) is a Singaporean-Chinese fusion dish that combines two bold flavour profiles: Korean fermented kimchi and Southeast Asian sambal chilli paste, stir-fried together with cooked white rice in a hot wok. The dish is a creative fusion offering found at seafood-focused restaurants in Singapore, such as The Seafood Market (By House of White Bee Hoon). The preparation involves wok-frying pre-cooked white rice with kimchi, sambal, egg, garlic, spring onion, and soy sauce over high heat to achieve the characteristic wok hei (smoky, charred aroma). The texture is slightly chewy from the rice grains, with crispy edges where the rice contacts the hot wok surface. The flavour is complex: tangy and sour from the kimchi fermentation, spicy and umami-rich from the sambal, savoury from soy sauce, and subtly smoky from wok cooking. The small size is marketed for 1–3 people as a shared side or individual main. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dominant food (approximately 172 kcal per 100 g, 26.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with notable sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g) from multiple high-sodium condiments. The medium glycemic index (estimated GI 64) and high glycemic load per serving make portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for those on low-sodium diets due to its hypertension concern level. It contains egg and soy allergens and is not halal-certified due to potential shrimp paste in sambal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for kimchi sambal fried rice exists in HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Foodo Chinese-style fried rice as the primary anchor, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy rice data and adjustments for kimchi and sambal components. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous fried rice entry. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct laboratory analysis for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on HPB Foodo 'Fried Rice (Chinese-style)' and USDA SR Legacy rice data; no direct laboratory analysis for this specific dish is available in approved sources. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries for fried rice. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for fried rice. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in the typical preparation of this dish using vegetable cooking oil. 5. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fried rice entry; no direct GI measurement for kimchi sambal fried rice exists in approved sources. 6. The food_group has been corrected from the input value 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' to 'Grains and staples' as this is a rice-based main dish. 7. Halal status is uncertain because commercial sambal pastes may contain shrimp paste (belacan); is_halal is set to false pending confirmation. 8. The small serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a shared small portion; actual restaurant serving weight may vary. 9. Glycemic load per serving = (64 × 92.75) / 100 ≈ 60.0, where 92.75 g is the carbohydrate content of a 350 g serving at 26.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.
